evals.name: retro-quick-reports
evals.prompt: Run a product retrospective for our Quick Reports feature. It shipped 6 weeks ago. Data: adoption is 34% (target was 50%), support tickets about reports dropped 60%, NPS for reporting went from 6.2 to 7.1. It shipped 1 week late due to a scope change (added PDF export mid-sprint).
evals.expected_output: A four-question retrospective with outcome data, delivery assessment, metrics quality review, and specific process improvements.
expectations: Q1 (Did it solve the problem?) uses the actual data provided — adoption at 34% vs 50% target, support tickets down 60%, NPS up from 6.2 to 7.1 — cherry-picking positive metrics while ignoring the adoption miss would produce a misleading retro
Q1 verdict reflects mixed results: adoption below target but support impact and NPS strongly positive — binary PASS/FAIL on a nuanced outcome misses the learning
Q2 (Did we build it as intended?) flags the 1-week delay and PDF export scope change — the scope change is the process issue that needs a corrective action, not just a footnote
Q3 (Were the metrics right?) evaluates whether adoption %, support tickets, and NPS were the right things to measure — metrics quality is often the most valuable retro finding
Q4 (What would we do differently?) produces specific, testable process changes — 'communicate better' is not a process change; 'scope changes after sprint start require PM + eng lead sign-off' is
PRODUCT.LOCAL.MD UPDATES section recommends at least one quality rule — a retro that produces zero configuration changes was a waste of everyone's time
Follow-on actions have owners and due dates — actionless retros are therapy sessions, not improvement tools

evals.name: retro-successful-launch
evals.prompt: Run a retro on our new onboarding flow. Shipped on time, all ACs met, adoption is 72% (target 60%), support tickets about onboarding down 45%. No scope changes during development. The team felt really good about this one.
evals.expected_output: A retrospective that documents success as rigorously as failure — capturing what went right so the process can be replicated.
expectations: Q1 verdict reflects that this exceeded targets — the retro captures success, not just problems
Q2 delivery assessment shows all-green metrics (on time, spec quality, scope maintained) — documenting what went right is what makes success repeatable
Q4 still produces at least one process observation — even successful features have learnings; the retro must not skip this section just because things went well
The retro does NOT only document what went wrong — per the NEVER rules, a retro that skips what went well fails to protect what works

evals.name: negative-no-outcome-data
evals.prompt: Run a retro on the search feature. We shipped it last week but I don't have any metrics yet.
evals.expected_output: The skill should push back — a retro without outcome data produces blame, not learning. Recommend waiting 4-12 weeks.
expectations: The skill flags that one week is too early for a meaningful retro — the NEVER rules prohibit running a retro without outcome data
Recommends waiting 4-12 weeks for outcome data to accumulate — rushing a retro without data produces opinion-based blame, not evidence-based improvement
May offer to run a delivery-focused Q2 review (on time? spec quality?) while deferring outcome questions

evals.name: negative-stakeholder-update
evals.prompt: Give me a stakeholder update on the search feature for this week's leadership meeting.
evals.expected_output: The skill should redirect to /stakeholder-update — this is a status update, not a retrospective.
expectations: The skill does NOT produce a retro when asked for a stakeholder update — status updates and retrospectives serve different audiences and purposes
